fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this HTML assertion utility we've been talking about. Basically it's a tool that lets devs write checks against rendered markup output without having to do all the messy DOM traversal stuff manually every time. The core idea is you feed it some HTML and tell it what you expect to find, and it either confirms the match or tells you what failed in a consistent, readable way.

We need it to handle the usual stuff — checking that certain text shows up, that specific element types exist, that links point to the right places, that CSS classes are on the right elements, that attribute values are exactly right. There's also a scoping thing — like the validation flow we used in that last auth module — where you can narrow down to a specific part of the document before running your checks. And we should support picking a specific element from a list by position, not just "find any match."

There's also that reusable charset check that keeps coming up in onboarding issues — we should make that a first-class thing so people stop reinventing it.

The output format needs to be totally deterministic and machine-parseable so CI pipelines can consume it without custom parsing logic. When something matches, say so cleanly. When it fails, tell us what condition failed without leaking internal error details.

Input should come in via stdin as structured data, one check per invocation.

One follow-up from the questions that came in: the adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin, and that object always includes a 'document' key with the raw HTML string. After that, the keys vary based on what kind of check is being asked for, using only 'text', 'selector', 'scope_selector', 'selection_selector', 'selection_mode', 'position', 'attribute', 'value', 'class', 'href', or 'charset'. The feature is inferred from the combination of keys that shows up in that JSON object, so there is no explicit 'feature' or 'command' key.

For output, keep it very strict. On success, it should be key=value lines printed to stdout. A matched result always starts with 'result=matched' on the first line, followed by the relevant context fields in the exact order for that feature. For example, a text presence check outputs: 'result=matched\ntext=<fragment>\n'. All output lines end with a newline, and the entire output ends with a trailing newline. On failure, the output must start with 'error=assertion_failed' on the first line, then 'condition=<condition_name>' on the second line, then the relevant selector or text fields. The condition names are fixed per feature: 'text_present' for text checks, 'selector_present' for selector checks, 'text_present_in_scope' for scoped text checks, 'attribute_value_matches' for attribute checks, 'class_present' for class checks, 'link_target_present' for href checks, and 'text_present_in_selection' for ordered selection text checks. Also just to be explicit, no internal exception details should appear in output.

On the scoping behavior, 'scope_selector' is what narrows the document to the subtree(s) matching that CSS selector before the main assertion runs. For selector presence in feature 2, the presence check runs within that scoped subtree. For text presence in feature 3, the text fragment is searched within the rendered content of the scoped elements. Same general behavior applies to the scope_selector mechanism described in features 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 — it narrows the document to a CSS-selected subtree before running the primary assertion, and its value must be echoed in the output as 'scope_selector=<value>'. When that field is present in output, it goes before the 'selector=' or 'text=' line.

And on picking one element out of a set, 'selection_mode' controls how the chosen element is taken from the results of 'selection_selector'. It accepts exactly 'first', 'last', and 'index'. 'first' picks the first matched element, 'last' picks the last matched element, and 'index' picks the zero-based element at the integer index given by the 'position' field. When these are present, the selection_mode and selection_selector values should be echoed in the output too, and 'position' is only required and only output when selection_mode is 'index'.

trigger_keywords: what is the charset check
how does feature 8 work
what is the reusable charset assertion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Feature 8 is a composite/reusable check that validates the document declares a specific character set. Internally it scopes to 'head', selects the first 'meta' element, and checks that the 'charset' attribute matches the provided value exactly. The input only needs the 'document' and 'charset' fields. On success it outputs 'result=matched\ncharset=<value>\n'. On failure it outputs the standard assertion_failed format with 'condition=attribute_value_matches' and 'attribute=charset' and 'value=<value>'.

trigger_keywords: does text presence use the DOM or raw string
is the text check against parsed HTML or raw markup
how does feature 1 search for text
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Feature 1 (text fragment presence) checks the raw rendered markup string directly for the literal fragment — it is NOT a DOM text-node search. This means it will match text appearing anywhere in the raw HTML string, including inside tag content, attribute values, or even malformed/recovered markup. The check is a simple substring containment test on the original document string as provided.

trigger_keywords: what is the output field order
do the output lines have a required order
does the order of key=value lines matter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, the order of output lines is strictly defined per feature and must match the contract exactly. The general order is: result/error line first, then condition (on failure), then scope_selector (if present), then selection_selector and selection_mode and position (if present), then the primary assertion field (selector, text, attribute+value, class, href, or charset). Deviating from this order will cause CI contract failures.

trigger_keywords: can the document be malformed HTML
does it handle broken markup
what happens with incomplete or invalid HTML
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes. The system must handle full HTML documents, fragments with multiple sibling root nodes, and malformed or partially broken markup gracefully. For text presence (feature 1) this is handled by raw string matching. For selector-based features, the HTML parser should apply recovery/repair logic so that queries still work against a best-effort parsed tree. Errors in parsing should not bubble up as unhandled exceptions or alter the output format.

trigger_keywords: how does the link check work
what is the href assertion
how does feature 6 find anchor elements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Feature 6 (link target presence) checks whether any anchor element ('a' tag) within the current document or selected fragment has an 'href' attribute that exactly matches the provided value. If 'scope_selector' and/or 'selection_selector' with 'selection_mode' are provided, the search is narrowed to that subtree or selected element first. On success output is 'result=matched\nhref=<value>\n' (plus any scope/selection fields before href). On failure, condition is 'link_target_present'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that reusable charset check that keeps coming up in onboarding issues
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 (feature8_reusable_charset_check.json) — a composite assertion that internally scopes to 'head', selects the first 'meta' element, and checks the 'charset' attribute; exposed externally only via a single 'charset' input field

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — each feature has a corresponding JSON file with concrete input/output examples that should clarify the expected behavior. If something still seems ambiguous after reviewing those, flag it and we can dig deeper.
